content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

My last piece argued that AI is the next rung on the abstraction ladder software engineering has always climbed: machine code to assembly, assembly to C, C to Java. That piece drew real engagement, and real pushback. Rather than answer each comment one at a time, I went and pulled the research together properly — across engineer surveys, controlled experiments, the economics of who becomes an engineer in the first place, and the closest historical precedent we have for what’s happening right now. Here is what a comprehensive look at the evidence actually shows.

Software Engineering Didn’t Need AI to Attract Career Changers
One thing the data makes clear immediately: people without a computer science background trying to become software engineers is not a new phenomenon, and AI didn’t start it. It has been happening at scale for over a decade, for a simple reason. Software engineering is a good job, and industry demand for it has consistently outpaced the supply of people trained to do it. IDC estimated the global shortage of full-time developers would rise from 1.4 million in 2021 to 4.0 million by 2025 (BairesDev, citing IDC). The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics still projects software developer employment to grow at roughly twice the average rate of all other occupations through the mid-2030s (BLS).
That gap is exactly why an entire education category exists to cross-train people into engineering roles. Green Fox Academy, founded in Budapest in 2015, runs an intensive four-month program built specifically for career changers, and reports 91% of graduates working as IT professionals afterward (Course Report). General Assembly reports a 95.7% placement rate for students who complete its career services track (Course Report). École 42, the tuition-free program that started in Paris, requires no diploma, runs no lectures, and has grown into a global network built entirely on project work and peer learning (42). None of these existed because of large language models. They exist because the industry needed more engineers than the traditional university pipeline could produce, and it built a parallel one. Career changers now make up an estimated 44% of the global coding bootcamp market, and that segment is growing faster than any other part of it (Mordor Intelligence).
AI didn’t open this door. It’s a door the industry has been holding open, deliberately, for years.

Juniors Are Not Alone With This, and They Shouldn’t Be
None of that erases what junior engineers are dealing with right now, and it would be dishonest to wave it away. Surveys of established engineers still lean positive — 76% optimistic in one survey of electrical and computer engineers, 70% not seeing AI as a threat to their job in Stack Overflow’s 2024 data (IEEE TEMS; IT Brew) — but that optimism concentrates among people who already have a job and a track record. Yale’s labor market research found that AI’s damage is landing almost entirely on people trying to get their first job (Yale Insights). Reporting on engineering graduates describes entry-level roles disappearing quietly, without a headline layoff to point to (Rest of World). A large multimethod study in the Journal of Applied Psychology found that exposure to automation reliably raises job insecurity, independent of how any individual feels about the technology (Yam et al., 2022). If you’re a junior engineer right now, you have a legitimate reason to be unhappy about your timing.
But here is the part that gets lost: engineers are not optional, even in a future with a genuinely superintelligent AI. Intelligence that generates code does not decide what’s worth building, does not carry accountability when production breaks, and does not sit in a room with a customer to work out what problem actually matters. It doesn’t work alone, because it isn’t built to work alone. The structural demand documented above doesn’t disappear because a tool got better at writing functions. What’s happening right now is a transition hiccup, not a verdict on the profession — and pretending otherwise doesn’t help anyone currently living through it.
The fix isn’t an individual junior engineer’s problem to solve alone, and it shouldn’t be treated as one. It’s a coordination problem between education and industry — the same coordination that built Green Fox Academy, General Assembly, and 42 to solve the last supply gap. That infrastructure already exists. It now needs to be pointed at teaching new engineers how to work alongside AI from day one, instead of leaving them to absorb it on the job while being measured against a benchmark that was never real to begin with.

Someone Else Is Climbing the Ladder
There’s a sharper version of the concern, too: that AI isn’t a new rung at all, it’s someone else grabbing the same rung you’re standing on. The research gives that argument more support than I expected.
A controlled MIT study published in Science found that ChatGPT cut task completion time by 40% and raised output quality by 18% on professional writing tasks. But the same study found the tool substituted for worker effort rather than complementing worker skill (Noy & Zhang, 2023). That’s close to an exact empirical description of someone else grabbing the ladder. The tool isn’t necessarily making the person more capable. It’s doing the part of the work that used to build the capability.
Compare that to the controlled study of GitHub Copilot, where developers completed a defined coding task 55.8% faster, with the largest gains concentrated among less experienced developers (Peng et al., 2023). Read generously, that’s amplification. Read less generously, it’s the same mechanism as the MIT study, just with better branding. The honest position is that both readings are supported by data, and the difference isn’t the tool. It’s whether an organization is using AI to remove drudgery around a skill someone is still building, or to remove the skill-building itself.

The Ladder Has Been Climbed Before
If there’s one place the evidence should carry the most weight, it’s here, because we’ve already run this experiment once, in public, over two decades, with the receipts still available.
When C and C++ gave way to Java in enterprise software, the argument was never as clean as “better language wins.” Essays like Moylan’s Case Against C argued that C’s manual memory model produced too many bugs to justify keeping it for large systems, decades before AI made anyone reconsider anything (Moylan). But a Brown University technical report cataloguing the actual friction points of the move — class design, operator overloading, memory management, portability — shows the transition wasn’t a clean upgrade. It was a genuine tradeoff, argued detail by detail, by engineers who had real reasons to be uneasy about giving up manual control for a runtime they couldn’t fully see inside (Lewandowski).
The productivity data from that era is more nuanced than either side of today’s AI argument wants it to be. Research on programming language productivity found that high-level languages could improve productivity, reliability, and comprehensibility by factors of five to fifteen over low-level languages — but the same research found that the variation between two programmers using the same language was often as large as the variation between languages (Coding Horror, citing Prechelt). A large-scale study of GitHub projects reached a parallel conclusion: process factors like team size and commit discipline predicted software defects better than which language a team used (Ray et al., 2014). The tool mattered less than who was using it and how — then, just as much as now.
The clearest illustration of where control actually went is Chronicle Software’s own account of building high-frequency trading systems — historically the one domain where nobody was supposed to trust a garbage-collected language — in Java (Chronicle Software). They didn’t win by treating Java as safe out of the box. They won by knowing exactly which parts of the Java runtime to route around and which to trust, a level of judgment a novice in either language doesn’t have. Control wasn’t eliminated by the higher-level tool. It moved to whoever understood the problem well enough to use the tool correctly — and that’s a skill that has to be built, deliberately, the same way Green Fox Academy and 42 build it today.
That’s the actual lesson of the last ladder, and it’s a more useful one than “the ladder always works out fine.” It worked out because engineers who understood the tradeoffs took ownership of them, in public, argument by argument, for years — while institutions built new pathways to train the next generation into that same judgment. Nothing about that required the transition to feel comfortable while it was happening.
None of this resolves into a tidy verdict, and it shouldn’t. The industry needs engineers structurally, has spent a decade building the infrastructure to make more of them, and is now asking that same infrastructure to absorb a technology that can make a junior engineer’s first two years feel unrecognizable. The last time this happened, with a language instead of a model, the resolution took longer than anyone wanted, and it depended on institutions choosing to solve it deliberately rather than waiting for it to sort itself out.
The open question isn’t whether the ladder holds. History says it does. The open question is whether education and industry choose to climb it together this time, on a timeline that doesn’t leave an entire generation of junior engineers standing at the bottom, wondering if there’s still a rung for them.
